title: Optimization of multisite reactions in complex compartmentalized media
abstract: In complex media, transport and geometric properties deeply influence the kinetics of random encounters between reactants. Here, we consider the situation where a random walker, moving in a regularly diffusing medium, has to reach and activate a target located inside a compartment characterized by fractal (obstructed) sub-diffusion. We focus on dual-site reactions, which end when two activation events occur within a given time window. Each activation event happens with a finite probability whenever the random walker visits the target. For weakly reactive targets, we demonstrate that the reaction time can be minimized for an optimal compartment size and can even be accelerated when compared to the same system without compartment. Our analytical predictions are validated through simulations of a random walker on a cubic lattice, where some sites inside the compartment are obstructed at the critical percolation threshold. Our theory illustrates the fact that adding a crowded compartment around a target, even if it slows down the motion in its vicinity, can accelerate the kinetics of complex reactions, especially for weakly reactive targets.
summary: This theoretical work analyzes how confining a reaction target within a compartment characterized by sub-diffusion can optimize the rate of multisite chemical reactions. By deriving scaling laws for mean first passage times, the authors show that the resulting reaction time can be minimized at an optimal compartment size, demonstrating kinetic acceleration despite local transport slowing.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the geometry and dynamics of a complex, compartmentalized medium influence the kinetics of multisite chemical reactions.
main_result: The presence of a crowded compartment can accelerate the reaction rate compared to an unconfined system, provided the target is weakly reactive, by optimizing the search process.
method: The authors derive analytical expressions for mean first passage times and reaction times by adapting arguments from random walk theory in fractal and diffusive media.
model_or_system: The model involves a random walker searching for two or more activation events (dual-site reactions) within a compartment exhibiting fractal sub-diffusion, contrasting this with classical 3D diffusion outside.
key_observables: Mean reaction time ($\langle T angle$), mean first passage time ($\langle 	au^*_r angle$), and the speed-up factor relative to the no-compartment case.
important_parameters: Compartment size ($R$), reaction probability ($p$), time window ($\Delta$), and diffusion exponents ($d_w, d_f$).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on the weakly reactive limit ($p \ll 1$) and the regime where the time window $\Delta$ is much smaller than the characteristic escape time ($\Delta \ll \langle 	au_\infty angle$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the optimization of $\langle T angle$ with respect to compartment radius ($R$) and the comparison of the acceleration factor ($\langle T angle / \langle T angle_{n.c.}$) versus reactivity ($p$).
paper_structure: The paper builds from calculating mean first passage times in confined/fractal media to deriving the reaction time for multisite events, culminating in showing the optimal size $R_m$ that minimizes $\langle T angle$.
why_interesting: The core concept of kinetic optimization via geometric confinement is relevant to understanding reaction rates in complex biological environments, which often exhibit fractal or disordered structures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Sketch of the system investigated in our simulations, consisting of a crowded sphere of radius R embedded in a regular lattice of side L (the surrounding medium). Light blue : free sites that are connected to the surrounding medium. Deep blue: obstructed sites. Black: free sites that are not connected to the surrounding medium. Red: target site. Here, the system is in two-dimensions, with q = 0.59, but our simulations are in three dimensions. (b) ⟨τ ∗ ∞⟩and ⟨τ ∗ 1 ⟩as a function of the compartment size. Symbols: simulations, with r = 23 for L = 50, r = 44 for L = 100, r = 88 for L = 200. Continuous black line: scaling (4), with α ≃3.4 (obtained from a fit). Dash-dotted black...

FIG. 2. Results for mobile targets, with q = 0.311. (a) Mean first passage time versus compartment radius R. (b) Valida- tion of the expression (17) for the activation time for weak reactivity (p < 0.1). (c) Reaction time versus R for different reactivities and ∆= 104 for L = 50, ∆= 105 for L = 100, ∆= 106 for L = 200 and reactivities p < 0.1. The line repre- sents the prediction (18).

FIG. 3. (a) Symbols: measured average number of sites of the compartment n(R) that are connected to the external medium. We also show the power-law behavior n(R) ∼Rdf with df ≃2.52 (red line) and the volume occupied by the compartment 4πR3/3 (dash-dotted line). (b) Symbols: mean square displacement for a random walker starting at the cen- ter of the compartment (r = 0), with L = 800 and R indicated in the legend. We also show the power-law ⟨r2⟩= t2/dw with dw ≃3.78 (black line) and the diffusive law ⟨r2⟩= t (blue line).
